explicit_text: I was born in Osaka in 1900, where my father was in the printing business serving churches. Both my parents were members of Temma Church in Osaka, and my father served as a deacon for many years. Although there were not many people from Osaka in the community here, I grew up there until I was 16 before moving to Tokyo.

explicit_text: When I was 16, I moved to Tokyo to live with my brother because my father could no longer support my schooling. My mother's cousin was a pastor at Bancho Church in Kojimachi, Tokyo, and he played a role in arranging my marriage. I lived in Tokyo from then on until I came to America after marrying my husband in 1923.

explicit_text: My husband worked as a tailor in Berkeley when we first came to America. Later, he started a dry cleaning business there around 1929, which we ran until the war broke out. Berkeley was where we lived and worked before the forced evacuation during World War II.

explicit_text: The Great Depression started around 1930, shortly after my husband began his dry cleaning business. We struggled financially and lived from hand to mouth during those years, but we managed to get some installment money back which helped us survive. It was a very hard time for us economically.

explicit_text: We were first sent to the Tanforan Assembly Center after the evacuation. Because we had three daughters, we were able to live in a new barracks rather than the stables. Although the floors were coarse and insects crawled through, we were better off than many others there.

explicit_text: At Tanforan, some people who believed Japan was winning the war were sent to Tule Lake. I was aware of the tensions and divisions among the Japanese in the camps, and those who were considered troublemakers or loyal to Japan were transferred there. We, however, were not sent to Tule Lake.

explicit_text: After Tanforan, we were sent to Topaz War Relocation Center in the desert. It was a scary experience arriving there after a three-day train ride, seeing nothing but desert around us. The windows were covered during the train ride through cities, so we were kept from seeing outside. Life there was harsh but we endured it.

explicit_text: During World War II, life was very difficult for us. My husband and I were forced to leave our dry cleaning business and were sent to internment camps. I worried about the war and the soldiers passing by, and I thought Japan had started a terrible thing. After the war ended in August 1945, we left the camps and tried to rebuild our lives.

explicit_text: I acquired American citizenship and believed we had to be loyal to America as citizens. However, I also felt that a Japanese person could never become completely American because of our spirit and heritage. My children were born American citizens, and I wanted them to do their duty as Americans while keeping their Japanese spirit.
